mechanism: Boswellic acids, including AKBA, are proposed to modulate inflammatory pathways (such as 5-lipoxygenase-related signaling). These mechanisms are biologically plausible and partly supported in human biomarker data, but clinical response depends on extract chemistry.
safetyNotes: Often tolerated in short-to-medium duration trials, with possible GI upset, reflux, diarrhea, or headache. Product quality and boswellic-acid standardization differ between brands, so adverse-effect risk may not be uniform.

sources.title: PubMed: Efficacy and Safety of Boswellia and Boswellia Extract for Osteoarthritis Patients

description: Boswellia serrata (Indian frankincense) resin is used in oral standardized extracts for joint symptoms and other inflammatory complaints. Meta-analyses indicate potential improvement in osteoarthritis pain and function, but study quality and extract composition vary substantially.
dosage: Clinical studies use varying standardized extract doses, so no single universal dose can be recommended across products and indications.
interactions: Direct high-quality interaction data are limited; use caution with anti-inflammatory and anticoagulant regimens until medication review is completed.
Use standardized single-ingredient extracts when possible to reduce uncertainty from mixed formulations.
legalStatus: Widely legal; used in supplements and Ayurvedic medicine
preparation: Typically used as gum-resin extracts in capsules/tablets standardized to boswellic acids.
region: Native to dry regions of India and adjoining areas; resin is harvested from Boswellia species in South Asia and the Horn of Africa/Arabian region depending on species.
sideEffects: Mild GI symptoms like nausea or diarrhea
summary: Boswellia serrata resin extracts are used mainly for osteoarthritis and inflammatory symptom support; clinical studies suggest modest pain-function benefit in some preparations.
